content.text: Claude Code and Claude Cowork are both agentic — they both execute multi-step tasks rather than simply generating text. But they serve different people doing different kinds of work, and the overlap between them is smaller than it first appears. Claude Code is a developer CLI built around repositories: it edits source files, runs tests, executes shell commands, and integrates with the tools engineers already use. Cowork is a knowledge-work platform built around projects: it writes documents, queries connected apps, runs sandboxed code, and automates recurring workflows for non-engineers and engineers alike.

attrs.alt: Claude Code (developer CLI, repo-focused) vs Claude Cowork (knowledge worker platform, project-focused)
attrs.title: Claude Code vs Claude Cowork Architecture

content.text: Claude Code is a command-line interface that runs inside a terminal, next to your code. Its context is a repository: it reads files from the working directory, understands the project structure via
, and makes edits that you review as diffs. The engineer remains in control of every file change — Claude proposes, the developer accepts or rejects.
with no scope restriction — it can touch any file in the repository.
— runs arbitrary commands in the host shell:
— pluggable custom tools that engineers configure to extend Claude Code's capabilities into proprietary APIs, databases, or CI systems.
that register reusable agent types invokable by name, enabling multi-agent workflows within a single session.
Claude Code does not persist memory between sessions beyond what the repository state provides (code, comments,
). Every Claude Code session starts fresh. There are no Projects, no standing instructions that survive outside the repository files, and no scheduled tasks.

Cowork is a desktop application (and web interface) that operates on goals rather than commands. Its context is a Project: persistent standing instructions, shared documents, and connector bindings that accumulate across sessions. Cowork's audience is broader than engineers — it is designed for anyone doing multi-step knowledge work that touches multiple apps.
— reads and writes within directories you explicitly grant. Not the full file system; not the full repository.
— Google Drive, Slack, GitHub, Jira, email, and others. Cowork reads and writes to these services using your delegated OAuth tokens, without touching your local shell.
— fetches live web content and performs searches as part of a task.
— Python, Node.js, and shell, isolated from the host OS. Useful for data transformation and analysis, but not for running your project's full test suite.
— recurring jobs and parallel child agents for large-scale workflows.
Both tools can execute code — but the execution environments are fundamentally different, and this matters for what each tool is suitable for.

Claude Code runs commands in your actual shell. It can run your test suite, invoke build tools, push to git, call Docker, and reach any service your machine has network access to. There is no sandbox. This is powerful but requires trust: you approve each command before it runs (or configure automatic approval for low-risk commands like read-only tools).

# Claude Code can run your actual test suite:
$ npm test
# or build your project:
$ cargo build --release
# or push to a remote:
$ git push origin main
This makes Claude Code the right tool for software engineering tasks where you need the real output: actual test failures on real code, actual build artifacts, actual deployment commands.

Cowork's code execution runs in a container isolated from your host OS. It cannot reach your file system outside the configured grant, cannot access services that require your local network configuration, and cannot run arbitrary system commands that would affect your machine. It is designed for data transformation, analysis scripts, and format conversion — not for running a project's native build toolchain.

# Cowork's sandboxed code is good for:
import json, csv

def normalize_data(raw):
    # transform a JSON payload into a CSV for the report
    rows = []
    for item in raw['entries']:
        rows.append([item['id'], item['name'], item['value']])
    return rows

# Not good for: running your actual test suite, deploying,
# or calling services that need local credentials.
Both tools can interact with GitHub, but through different mechanisms with different capabilities.
interacts with GitHub by running git commands in your host shell and using MCP servers if configured. It can commit, branch, push, and create pull requests via the
CLI. It has full access to your local git state, including staged changes, stash, and commit history. Code-level operations — rebasing, cherry-picking, comparing diffs across branches — are natural.

interacts with GitHub via an OAuth connector. It can read issues, create issues, post comments, read PR descriptions, and update labels. It cannot clone repositories, run git commands, or operate on local working tree state. Cowork's GitHub interaction is at the project management layer — the kind of GitHub work that a product manager or a DevOps engineer does in the browser.

If you want Claude to fix a bug, run the tests, and open a PR — use Claude Code. If you want Claude to triage the 40 open issues in a repository, apply labels, and post a summary to Slack — use Cowork.
When Claude Code Is the Right Choice
You are writing, refactoring, or debugging code and need the actual test output to validate correctness.
The task requires understanding the repository's structure — imports, types, test coverage, build configuration.
You need to run the project's native build toolchain (webpack, cargo, gradle, make) and verify its output.
You want to commit, push, and open a PR — operations that require a real git working tree.
You are extending Claude Code itself via custom MCP servers or skills that integrate with engineering infrastructure.
When Cowork Is the Right Choice
The task crosses multiple apps — reading from Google Drive, processing, and writing to Slack or email.
The task should run on a schedule (weekly report, daily digest) without engineer intervention.
Non-engineers need access to the same AI-powered workflow — a product manager running the same research template as their engineer colleague.
The task is GitHub project management (issue triage, label assignment, PR summaries) rather than code-level work.
The team needs shared context, shared documents, and shared standing instructions that persist across many sessions.
The Overlap Zone: When Either Works
A few use cases sit squarely at the boundary. Engineers often find themselves reaching for either tool depending on where they are in their workflow:
Reads source code, generates docs, writes to repo
Reads docs folder, writes Markdown, posts to Drive
Reads git diff, writes PR description
Reads PR via GitHub connector, posts Slack summary
Reads GitHub + Jira via connectors, scheduled, posts to Slack
In the overlap zone, the tiebreakers are: Does it recur? (Cowork.) Does it need the local git working tree? (Claude Code.) Does a non-engineer need to run it too? (Cowork.)
Claude Code and Cowork are complementary in an engineering team context. A practical pattern:
to implement a feature: edits code, runs tests, opens a PR.
runs nightly via the GitHub connector: reads all open PRs, identifies those awaiting review, and posts a summary to the team's Slack channel.
to track feature progress: reads GitHub issues and Jira tickets via connectors, writes a weekly stakeholder update to Google Drive, and sends it as an email.

Each tool handles the work it is designed for. They share the GitHub connector as the integration point — Claude Code writes to GitHub at the code level; Cowork reads from GitHub at the project management level.
How each tool maintains context across interactions is one of the starkest differences — and the one that most determines whether a workflow scales.
Every Claude Code session starts by loading the working directory and the
file. That is the complete standing memory. When the session ends, what persists is whatever you committed to the repository — code, comments, updated

files. There is no external store. If you want Claude to remember something across sessions, you write it into the repo. This is durable and version-controlled, but it also means memory is only as good as your discipline in maintaining

# CLAUDE.md — the only cross-session memory in Claude Code

## Project conventions
- Use snake_case for file names, camelCase for variables
- Run `npm test` before committing
- API keys live in .env.local (never commit)

## Current work
- Auth module being refactored (see /src/auth/)
- Do not touch /src/legacy/ without asking

This model fits software development well. The repository is naturally the authoritative record of a software project, and placing Claude's standing instructions in the same repo means they are reviewed, diffed, and versioned alongside the code.

Cowork maintains a persistent Project for each team use case. A Project stores standing instructions, uploaded reference documents, connector bindings, and the outputs of past runs. Every session that opens the Project inherits all of this automatically — no need to re-explain the task, re-attach files, or reconfigure connectors.
— plain text describing the team's workflow, tone, output format, and constraints.
— PDFs, spreadsheets, or other files uploaded to the Project that every session can read.
— OAuth tokens associated with the Project, so every session has access to Drive, Slack, GitHub without re-authenticating.
— outputs generated by past sessions (reports, analyses) accessible to future sessions.
This model fits recurring knowledge work. The same team member can hand off a task to a different team member and the new person inherits all context automatically — no re-briefing, no file hunting.

The security boundaries of each tool reflect their different threat models. Claude Code operates in an environment the engineer controls (their own machine, their own credentials). Cowork operates in a multi-user, cloud-hosted environment where actions have organizational visibility.
Local env vars (.env files); engineer's responsibility
OAuth tokens stored by Cowork; scoped to connector actions
Source code and shell output included in prompts
File contents and connector response bodies included in prompts
Usage logs, audit trails, permission grants/revokes
Local repo + any shell commands run (recoverable via git)
Connector-scoped writes (Slack messages, Drive files, GitHub issues)

The key insight is that both tools expose real write access to real systems, but through different vectors. Claude Code's exposure is local (shell, files); Cowork's exposure is organizational (SaaS writes visible to colleagues). Neither is inherently riskier — the relevant question is which surfaces matter for your threat model.
Both tools support multi-agent patterns, but their implementations differ in important ways.
Claude Code uses skills (Markdown files in

) to define reusable agent types. A skill can spawn subagents — child Claude instances that each operate on a subset of the task. The primary use case is engineering: one subagent reviews code for security issues, another runs linting, a third updates documentation. Because subagents share access to the same local file system (via worktrees or shared directories), they can exchange code artifacts directly.

tool in Claude Code) allow deterministic orchestration: pipeline, parallel, and conditional fan-out are defined in JavaScript and run as structured agent pipelines. This is engineered parallelism — the programmer defines the structure.

Cowork supports subagent spawning for large-scale knowledge workflows. A parent agent can break a task into subtasks, delegate each to a child agent, and aggregate results. For example, a research task might spawn one subagent per company to profile, then a synthesis agent to compare findings. Subagents share Project context — they all see the same standing instructions, connector bindings, and reference documents.

Unlike Claude Code, subagents in Cowork are model-driven: the parent agent decides when to spawn and what to delegate. This is more flexible for open-ended research but less predictable than a scripted workflow.
The two tools have very different time-to-first-useful-output curves.
Anthropic API key or claude.ai login
15–30 min (create Project, write instructions, connect apps)
Faster than the first (Project context fully loaded)
They clone the repo; CLAUDE.md gives them context
Admin invites them to Project; they inherit all context

Claude Code has a lower initial barrier but does not accumulate value over time in the way Cowork does. A Cowork Project that has been running for three months — with refined standing instructions, accumulated reference documents, and a stable connector configuration — will outperform a fresh-start session dramatically. Claude Code's ceiling is determined by the quality of the repository and the
; Cowork's ceiling is determined by the quality of the Project.
Using Claude Code for recurring non-code workflows.

A weekly competitive analysis or stakeholder report is not a software engineering task. It needs connectors, scheduling, and shared output — all Cowork features. Running it as a one-off Claude Code session every Friday requires manual re-briefing each time.
Using Cowork to fix bugs or run tests.
Cowork cannot run your project's native test suite or push commits. Asking Cowork to "fix the failing CI" will produce a document describing what might be wrong — not a working fix in your codebase.
Conflating sandboxed code execution with host shell access.

Cowork's Python sandbox cannot install packages with system dependencies, cannot reach your company's internal services, and cannot run docker. Treating it like a full development environment leads to frustration.
Expecting Claude Code to remember context across sessions.
Every Claude Code session is cold-started. If you rely on Claude Code for an ongoing research task and do not commit context to
, you must re-explain the task each time. Use a Cowork Project for tasks that accumulate context.
Assuming Cowork's GitHub connector replaces Claude Code's git integration.

The GitHub OAuth connector reads and writes issues, PRs, and comments. It cannot clone a repository, run git commands, view a local diff, or push commits. If the task is code-level, the connector is the wrong entry point.
When unsure which surface to use, run through these eight questions. The first definitive answer stops the evaluation:
Does the task require running tests, compiling code, or executing shell commands on your machine?
Does it require editing source files, creating commits, or working with the local git working tree?
Does it need to read or write to cloud services (Drive, Slack, Jira, Gmail)?
Does it need to run on a schedule without anyone triggering it?
Does a non-engineer need to run it or see its output?
Does it need to remember context across sessions without relying on a repository?
Does the team need to share the workflow and its outputs?
Is it a one-off engineering task with no recurring value?
→ Either works; Claude Code is faster to start.

Claude Code owns the engineering loop: repository context, host shell, git, test runners, and MCP-extended integrations — all optimised for engineers who want fine-grained control over code changes. Cowork owns the knowledge-work loop: project memory, OAuth connectors, scheduled tasks, and team sharing — optimised for multi-step workflows that span apps and people. An engineer doing code work reaches for Claude Code; the same engineer running a cross-functional recurring process reaches for Cowork. Both are agentic, both execute multi-step plans — but the task shape, the tool palette, and the intended user are distinct.
